5: The sign given to Hezekiah after YHWH grants him fifteen additional years — ûḇayyôm haššᵉlîšî taʿăleh bêt YHWH, ‘and on the third day you shall go up to the house of YHWH’ — embeds within it the third-day pattern that Paul identifies in 1 Corinthians 15:4 as the scriptural framework for Christ’s resurrection: kai hoti ēgerthē tē hēmera tē tritē kata tas graphas — ‘and that he was raised on the third day in accordance with the Scriptures.’ The ‘Scriptures’ Paul cites do not name a single text; they describe a pattern present across multiple OT narratives. Hezekiah’s recovery from mortal illness and his third-day ascent to the house of YHWH is one of the OT’s clearest instances of this pattern: a man under sentence of death (v1: ‘you shall die; you shall not recover’), granted life by divine reversal, vindicated publicly on the third day in the sanctuary. Hosea 6:2 names the same structure: yᵉḥayyēnû miyyōmāyim bayyôm haššᵉlîšî yᵉqîmēnû wᵉniḥyeh lᵉpānāyw — ‘after two days he will revive us; on the third day he will raise us up, that we may live before him.’ The conjunction of ‘third day’ and ‘before YHWH / house of YHWH’ in both Hosea 6:2 and 2 Kings 20:5 establishes the pattern: the restored life that comes after judgment is life oriented toward YHWH’s presence. Jesus’s resurrection on the third day and his ascent to the Father (John 20:17: ‘I am ascending to my Father and your Father’) fulfills both the death-reversal and the sanctuary-ascent that Hezekiah’s healing anticipates.
1: Hezekiah becomes mortally ill — "at the point of death." The man who trusted YHWH against Sennacherib now faces an enemy that no imperial might can address: his own mortality. Isaiah comes with the divine verdict: "Put your house in order; you will die, not live." Even the faithful Davidic king is mortal; even the king whom YHWH protected against empire cannot escape death without divine intervention. Christ alone is "the firstborn from the dead" (Colossians 1:18) — the one for whom death is not the final verdict but the penultimate chapter.
2: Hezekiah turns his face to the wall and prays. The physical turning away from human witnesses — turning toward YHWH alone — is the posture of desperate prayer. The wall is the boundary between the visible and the invisible; Hezekiah faces the invisible. Prayer in extremity that turns away from the available human resources and toward YHWH alone is the posture Christ models in Gethsemane (Matthew 26:39: "going a little farther, he fell on his face and prayed").
3: "Remember how I have walked before you faithfully and wholeheartedly, and have done what is good in your sight." Hezekiah's prayer appeals to his record. The contrast with Christ's Gethsemane prayer is instructive: Jesus does not appeal to his own faithfulness but prays "your will be done" — not because he has no faithfulness to appeal to, but because his prayer is not a transaction but a surrender. Hezekiah's self-assessment is accurate (v3b: "he wept bitterly"); Christ's prayer is theologically deeper because it releases the outcome entirely to the Father's will.
4: The word of YHWH comes to Isaiah before he has even left the middle courtyard. The response is immediate — more immediate than normal prophetic channels. YHWH's answer to Hezekiah's prayer is not delayed. The immediacy of the divine response to the faithful king's prayer in extremity is a type of the Father's readiness to act: "Call upon me in the day of trouble; I will deliver you, and you shall glorify me" (Psalms 50:15). The resurrection is YHWH's immediate reversal of the verdict of death on his Son.
6: "I will add fifteen more years to your life." YHWH gives more than the minimum — not just healing but a specific, generous extension. "And I will rescue you and this city from the hand of the king of Assyria, and I will defend this city." The individual healing is embedded in the corporate promise; Hezekiah's life and Jerusalem's protection are linked. Christ's resurrection similarly produces more than personal survival: it generates the church, fills the disciples with the Spirit, and opens salvation to the nations (Acts 2).
7: A poultice of figs applied to the boil — physical medicine within the miraculous healing. The use of ordinary means alongside divine action is characteristic of YHWH's economy: he works through burning bushes and donkeys and fish-full nets and fig poultices. The incarnation is the supreme version of this: God healing the world through the most ordinary of means — a human body, a carpenter from Galilee, a meal of bread and wine, water of baptism.
8: Hezekiah asks for a sign. The request for a sign after a divine promise is not unbelief (compare Gideon, Judges 6:36–40; compare Mary, Luke 1:34) — it is the proper seeking of a concrete divine marker that can anchor ongoing trust. Isaiah offers the choice of shadow going forward or backward — the sign's form leaves YHWH to determine the harder option. The giving of signs is YHWH's accommodation to human need for concrete anchors of faith; the resurrection is the ultimate sign, given not on request but in advance of the disciples' full understanding.
9: The shadow on the sundial can advance ten steps or go backward ten steps — Isaiah offers the choice. The reversal of time's direction (the shadow going backward) is the harder, more dramatic option. It is also the more Christological: the resurrection is not just time extended but time reversed — death undone, not merely postponed. The backward movement of the shadow is a type of the resurrection's retroactive logic: what was done is undone; what was closed is opened; what went forward into death goes back into life.
10: Hezekiah chooses the shadow going backward — "it is easy for it to go forward; rather, let it go back ten steps." The choice reveals wisdom: he recognizes that the more dramatic sign is the more demanding request, and he asks for it. The willingness to ask for the harder thing — not just the extension of the normal but the reversal of the natural — is the posture of faith that matches the magnitude of the promise. The resurrection of Christ is the ultimate "backward" sign: the reversal of the natural order of death at the point of maximum human certainty that death had won.
11: YHWH makes the shadow go back ten steps on the staircase of Ahaz. The reversal of the shadow is YHWH's sovereignty over the created order — specifically over time, as marked by the movement of light. The darkness that covered the earth at the crucifixion (Mark 15:33) and the dawn of the resurrection morning are the cosmic counterparts: the created order responds to the death and resurrection of the Creator's Son. YHWH's governance of light and shadow from Ahaz's sundial to Golgotha's darkness is a continuous demonstration of the same sovereign power.
12: Merodach-Baladan of Babylon sends letters and a gift — having heard of Hezekiah's illness and recovery. The Babylonian king's interest is diplomatic: the anti-Assyrian coalition is gathering. Hezekiah's miraculous recovery has given him a new reputation in the international arena. The verse is transitional — introducing the Babylonian threat that will come long after Hezekiah's death. The narrative places the future threat's seed in the moment of Hezekiah's greatest miraculous blessing.
13: Hezekiah shows the Babylonian envoys everything — "the silver, the gold, the spices, the fine oil, his armory, all that was found in his storehouses. There was nothing in his palace or in all his realm that Hezekiah did not show them." The word "nothing" is the condemnation. The king who trusted YHWH against Sennacherib surrenders the temple and palace treasures to pride before the Babylonian delegation. The great failure of Hezekiah is not military but spiritual: he uses divine gifts for self-glorification rather than for YHWH's purposes. The contrast with Christ, who took "no account of being equal with God" and did not use divine gifts for self-display (Philippians 2:6–7), is acute.
14: Isaiah comes immediately to Hezekiah: "What did these men say? Where did they come from?" The prophetic interrogation is not information-gathering but the form of the judgment that follows. Isaiah already knows; the question is Hezekiah's moment to recognize what he has done. "They came from Babylon" — the answer names the coming threat. The prophet's arrival after the diplomatic failure is the word of God entering the situation of human pride to pronounce its consequences.
15: Hezekiah's candid answer: "They saw everything in my palace. There was nothing among my storehouses that I did not show them." The complete disclosure — given to Isaiah as it was given to the Babylonians — is both honest and damning. The king who showed everything has nothing hidden from the prophet. The transparency that should have governed Hezekiah's relationship with YHWH has instead governed his diplomatic self-presentation. The gospel reverses this: "Nothing is hidden that will not be revealed" (Luke 12:2) — not as condemnation but as the ultimate transparency of a community that lives before God.
16: "Then Isaiah said to Hezekiah: Listen to the word of the LORD." The formula introduces judgment in prophetic speech: the narrative slows, the divine voice is about to speak consequence. Hezekiah, who heard the word of the LORD through Isaiah in rescue (2 Kings 19:6–7, 20–34), now hears it in warning. The word of YHWH addresses both deliverance and discipline; neither exhausts its reach. Christ himself pronounces both grace and judgment — the same mouth that says "come to me" also says "depart from me" (Matthew 11:28; 2 Kings 7:23).
17: "Look — days are coming when everything in your palace, and everything your ancestors have stored up to this day, will be carried off to Babylon; nothing will be left, says the LORD." The Babylonian exile — over a century away — is prophesied precisely and specifically from the moment of Hezekiah's pride. The connection between Hezekiah's display and the future emptying of Jerusalem is direct: what he showed becomes what is taken. The principle of Deuteronomic consequence operates even at the temporal distance of generations. Christ takes this principle into himself: he absorbs the consequence, becoming the one who is "emptied" (Philippians 2:7: ἐκένωσεν) so his people need not be.
18: "And some of your own descendants will be taken away and will become eunuchs in the palace of the king of Babylon." This is fulfilled in Daniel and his companions (Daniel 1:3–7). The prophecy moves from the general (palace treasures) to the personal (Hezekiah's own descendants). The long reach of covenant consequence is demonstrated: Hezekiah's pride in 701 BC produces captive royal descendants in 605–586 BC. Yet even in that captivity, YHWH's purposes advance: Daniel in Babylon becomes the vehicle of divine revelation; Joseph in Egypt became the vehicle of preservation. YHWH's faithfulness works through the consequences of human failure as well as through human obedience.
19: Hezekiah's response: "The word of the LORD that you have spoken is good … will there not be peace and security in my lifetime?" The troubling element is the second sentence — "at least during my lifetime there will be peace." Hezekiah accepts future punishment as long as his own era is undisturbed. The contrast with vicarious suffering is stark: the faithful servant accepts present suffering for future others' benefit; Hezekiah accepts future suffering as long as his own present is undisturbed. Christ's posture is the inverse: he accepts immediate death — the cross — so that future generations are permanently secured. Hezekiah's acquiescence to futurity is the anti-type of Christ's embrace of the present for the sake of all who come after.
20: Hezekiah's regnal close notes his great engineering achievement: the pool and tunnel that brought water into Jerusalem. The Siloam tunnel — archaeologically verified, with the workers' inscription found in 1880 — is a genuine monument to human ingenuity and Hezekiah's foresight. The tunnel that brings water into the city to sustain it in siege is a shadow of Christ, who declares "let anyone who is thirsty come to me" (John 7:37–38) — the living water that flows not through rock but through the one who is himself the source.
21: Hezekiah dies, and Manasseh — the worst king in Judah's history — reigns in his place. The contrast is the narrative's final word on the limits of even the best earthly king: his succession is beyond his control. Hezekiah's faith and reform cannot be transmitted by heredity; Manasseh will undo everything and add more. Only Christ's kingdom produces successors of the same character as the king, because the king himself indwells his successors through the Spirit (John 14:17; 2 Kings 15:4). The failure of Hezekiah's succession is the permanent argument for why the kingdom needs a different king — one who does not merely reign for twenty-nine years and die, but reigns forever (Isaiah 9:7; Luke 1:33).
